problem_statement.title: stop out of bounds `iterationrange` for xgboost
problem_statement.body:
To close #1316 

EDIT: Adding a bit more context. This was technically an issue before too, where asking out of bounds also gave different results. However, it now causes an error, hence why we have to fix it

``` r
library(parsnip)
library(tune)
library(workflows)
library(recipes)
library(rsample)
library(yardstick)

# Create simple data
set.seed(42)
data <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(50),
  x2 = rnorm(50),
  y = rnorm(50)
)

# Create recipe
rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = data)

# Problematic model specification
xgb_spec <- boost_tree(
  trees = tune(),  # This parameter causes the issue
  engine = "xgboost", 
  mode = "regression"
)

# Create workflow
wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(rec) %>%
  add_model(xgb_spec)

# Create CV folds
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(data, v = 2)

# Simple grid
grid <- data.frame(trees = c(5, 10))

# This produces the error:
tune_grid(
  wf,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  grid = grid,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)
#> # Tuning results
#> # 2-fold cross-validation 
#> # A tibble: 2 × 4
#>   splits          id    .metrics         .notes          
#>   <list>          <chr> <list>           <list>          
#> 1 <split [25/25]> Fold1 <tibble [2 × 5]> <tibble [0 × 4]>
#> 2 <split [25/25]> Fold2 <tibble [2 × 5]> <tibble [0 × 4]>
```
problem_statement.issue_title: boost_tree() with new xgboost and trees = tune() fails with "Out of range for tree layers"
problem_statement.issue_body:
## The problem

When using `parsnip::boost_tree()` with `trees = tune()` and the new XGBoost package, hyperparameter tuning with `tune::tune_grid()` consistently fails with errors of the type

```
→ A | error:   ℹ In index: 1.
               Caused by error in `predict.xgb.Booster()`:
               ! [10:23:27] src/gbm/gbtree.h:124: Check failed: end <= model.BoostedRounds() (11 vs. 10) : Out of range for tree layers.
```

This appears to be an indexing issue where parsnip is requesting more trees than the model actually contains during the prediction phase of cross-validation.

## Reproducible example

```
library(parsnip)
library(tune)
library(workflows)
library(recipes)
library(rsample)
library(yardstick)

# Create simple data
set.seed(42)
data <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(50),
  x2 = rnorm(50),
  y = rnorm(50)
)

# Create recipe
rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = data)

# Problematic model specification
xgb_spec <- boost_tree(
  trees = tune(),  # This parameter causes the issue
  engine = "xgboost", 
  mode = "regression"
)

# Create workflow
wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(rec) %>%
  add_model(xgb_spec)

# Create CV folds
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(data, v = 2)

# Simple grid
grid <- data.frame(trees = c(5, 10))

# This produces the error:
result <- tune_grid(
  wf,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  grid = grid,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)
```
This gives the output:

```
[1] "XGBoost version:3.1.2.1"
Attempting to tune XGBoost with trees parameter...
This should produce the 'Out of range for tree layers' error

i Fold1: preprocessor 1/1
i Fold1: preprocessor 1/1, model 1/1
i Fold1: preprocessor 1/1, model 1/1 (predictions)
i Fold1: preprocessor 1/1, model 1/1 (predictions)
→ A | error:   ℹ In index: 1.
               Caused by error in `predict.xgb.Booster()`:
               ! [10:23:27] src/gbm/gbtree.h:124: Check failed: end <= model.BoostedRounds() (11 vs. 10) : Out of range for tree layers.
               Stack trace:
                 [bt] (0) /home/jullum/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.3/xgboost/libs/xgboost.so(+0x7d55f) [0x7fa05c0c355f]
                 [bt] (1) /home/jullum/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.3/xgboost/libs/xgboost.so(+0x1ed10c) [0x7fa05c23310c]
                 [bt] (2) /home/jullum/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.3/xgboost/libs/xgboost.so(+0x1df2b2) [0x7fa05c2252b2]
                 [bt] (3) /home/jullum/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.3/xgboost/libs/xgboost.so(+0x460bec) [0x7fa05c4a6bec]
                 [bt] (4) /home/jullum/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.3/xgboost/libs/xgboost.so(XGBoosterPredictFromDMatrix+0x3bc) [0x7fa05c5a850c]
                 [bt] (5) /home/jullum/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.3/xgboost/libs/xgboost.so(+0x7a7d1) [0x7fa05c0c07d1]
                 [bt] (6) /usr/lib/R/lib/libR.so(+0x10269e) [0x7fa0659f669e]
                 [bt] (7) /usr/lib/R/lib/libR.so(+0x14740c) [0x7fa065a3b40c]
                 [bt] (8) /usr/lib/R/lib/libR.so(Rf_eval+0x180) [0x7fa065a4
i Fold2: preprocessor 1/1
i Fold2: preprocessor 1/1, model 1/1
i Fold2: preprocessor 1/1, model 1/1 (predictions)
i Fold2: preprocessor 1/1, model 1/1 (predictions)

Tuning results:
# A tibble: 1 × 7
  trees .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <dbl> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1     5 rmse    standard    1.24     2  0.0736 pre0_mod1_post0
```

I.e. the model with 10 trees fails and is not included in the comparison. 
Using a longer set of trees, it always gives an error for the highest number. 

I have verified that the above runs fine with the old xgboost version (1.7.7.1), so I believe this is caused a bug in how the number of trees are extracted from the new xgboost version. There might be an issue with the iterationrange here? https://github.com/tidymodels/parsnip/blob/77e08226814f7f51849450671ca1600b00e630aa/R/boost_tree.R#L653-L668

Here is my sessionInfo:


patch:
diff --git a/.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml b/.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml
index 135e8834f..94a0bedc1 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml
+++ b/.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml
@@ -56,7 +56,7 @@ jobs:
 
       - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r-dependencies@v2
         with:
-          extra-packages: any::rcmdcheck
+          extra-packages: any::rcmdcheck, xgboost=?ignore-before-r=4.3.0
           needs: check
 
       - name: Install dev reticulate
diff --git a/NEWS.md b/NEWS.md
index 0282b2f30..7966795a2 100644
--- a/NEWS.md
+++ b/NEWS.md
@@ -1,5 +1,7 @@
 # parsnip (development version)
 
+* Fixed bug where xgboost mdoels would fail to predict when `trees` matched number of trees in model. (#1316)
+
 # parsnip 1.4.0
 
 * Fixes issue with running predictions for Decision Trees in Spark (#1309)
diff --git a/R/boost_tree.R b/R/boost_tree.R
index beb11b46f..b15c6f6af 100644
--- a/R/boost_tree.R
+++ b/R/boost_tree.R
@@ -653,16 +653,19 @@ multi_predict._xgb.Booster <-
 xgb_by_tree <- function(tree, object, new_data, type, ...) {
   rlang::check_installed("xgboost")
   if (utils::packageVersion("xgboost") >= "2.0.0.0") {
+    # avoid out of range
+    range_end <- min(nrow(attr(object$fit, "evaluation_log")), tree + 1)
+
     pred <- xgb_predict(
       object$fit,
       new_data = new_data,
-      iterationrange = c(1, tree + 1)
+      iterationrange = c(1, range_end)
     )
   } else {
     pred <- xgb_predict(
       object$fit,
       new_data = new_data,
-      iterationrange = c(1, tree + 1),
+      iterationrange = c(1, min(object$fit$niter, tree + 1)),
       ntreelimit = NULL
     )
   }
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/testthat/test-boost_tree_xgboost.R b/tests/testthat/test-boost_tree_xgboost.R
index cdc60849c..064322dd8 100644
--- a/tests/testthat/test-boost_tree_xgboost.R
+++ b/tests/testthat/test-boost_tree_xgboost.R
@@ -337,6 +337,30 @@ test_that('submodel prediction', {
   )
 })
 
+test_that("submodel prediction doesn't error if trees match number of trees (#1316)", {
+  skip_if_not_installed("xgboost")
+  skip_on_cran()
+
+  ctrl$verbosity <- 0L
+
+  reg_fit <-
+    boost_tree(trees = 20, mode = "regression") |>
+    set_engine("xgboost") |>
+    fit(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars[-(1:4), ])
+
+  x <- xgboost::xgb.DMatrix(as.matrix(mtcars[1:4, -1]))
+
+  pruned_pred <- predict(
+    extract_fit_engine(reg_fit),
+    x,
+    iterationrange = c(1, 20)
+  )
+
+  mp_res <- multi_predict(reg_fit, new_data = mtcars[1:4, -1], trees = 20)
+  mp_res <- do.call("rbind", mp_res$.pred)
+  expect_equal(mp_res[[".pred"]], pruned_pred)
+})
+
 # ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 
 test_that('validation sets', {

fail_to_pass: test_that("submodel prediction doesn't error if trees match number of trees (#1316)", { ... }) - verifies that multi_predict with trees=20 works on a model trained with 20 trees
pass_to_pass: Existing submodel prediction tests should continue to pass
Tests for predictions with trees < model's tree count should work

key_changes: Add bounds checking using min() to prevent iterationrange from exceeding model's actual tree count
Handle both xgboost >= 2.0.0.0 (use evaluation_log) and older versions (use niter)
The iterationrange upper bound should be min(actual_iterations, tree + 1)
potential_pitfalls: Off-by-one errors: iterationrange is 1-indexed with exclusive upper bound (c(1, n+1) gives n iterations)
Not handling both xgboost version cases (>= 2.0.0.0 and older)
Using wrong attribute to determine number of iterations
Forgetting that trees parameter from user is 0-indexed conceptually but iterationrange needs adjustment

instruction: Fix the bug in the `xgb_by_tree()` function where requesting predictions with `trees` parameter equal to the number of trees in the model causes an 'Out of range for tree layers' error in xgboost. The function should properly bound the `iterationrange` parameter to avoid requesting more iterations than the model contains. This affects the `multi_predict` functionality when users request predictions for a specific number of trees that matches or exceeds what was trained.
context: The `xgb_by_tree()` function in R/boost_tree.R handles per-tree predictions for xgboost models. The function uses `iterationrange` parameter which is 1-indexed with exclusive upper bound. The bug occurs when `tree + 1` exceeds the actual number of trees in the model. For xgboost >= 2.0.0.0, the number of iterations can be obtained via `nrow(attr(object$fit, "evaluation_log"))`. For older versions, use `object$fit$niter`. The fix requires using `min()` to bound the range_end appropriately.
